lines.text: Chapter one, section seven. Scenario: Dodging a Debt.
Now we come to what is arguably the most important scenario in the chapter — a game that, unlike the previous examples, has no equilibrium involving pure strategies at all.
The scenario: Christina owes David one hundred dollars.
It's Saturday night, and both of them independently plan to go to one of two poker rooms — call them Room A and Room B.
If they end up at the same room, Christina will have to pay David the hundred dollars she owes him.
David's goal is to find Christina and collect.
Christina's goal is to avoid David and not pay.
These interests are completely opposed — whatever is good for David is bad for Christina, and vice versa.
The payoff grid for this game is clean and symmetric.
If both go to Room A, David collects his hundred dollars: Christina's payoff is negative one hundred, David's is positive one hundred.
If Christina goes to Room A but David goes to Room B, they miss each other: Christina's payoff is zero, David's is zero.
If Christina goes to Room B and David goes to Room A, they again miss each other: Christina zero, David zero.
If both go to Room B, David again collects: Christina is negative one hundred, David is positive one hundred.
The grid has four cells and only two distinct outcomes: they meet, in which case Christina pays and David receives, or they don't meet, in which case both get zero.
Now for the four questions this scenario poses.
The first question asks you to explain or demonstrate why no single cell in this grid represents an equilibrium.
In other words, why is there no pure-strategy equilibrium for this game?
The second question asks: given that no pure-strategy equilibrium exists, what is the equilibrium for this game?
This is a more challenging question that requires you to think beyond always-do-one-thing strategies.
The third question asks: at that equilibrium, what is each player's expected value?
And the fourth asks you to prove that neither player has any incentive to deviate from the equilibrium strategy.
Let's set up the intuition before you try to work through the answers.
Think about what would happen if Christina simply decided to always go to Room A.
If David knows that's her strategy, he should always go to Room A too — then he always collects.
But if David always goes to Room A, Christina should switch to Room B — then she never pays.
But if Christina always goes to Room B, David should follow her there.
And if David always goes to Room B, Christina should go to Room A.
No matter which room either player commits to, their opponent has a response that beats them.
This is a fundamentally different structure from the driving game or the movie scenario, where we could find stable resting points.
Here, every pure-strategy combination creates a situation where one player is unhappy and wants to change.
The only way out of this cycle is to make your choice unpredictable — to randomize.
If Christina can't be predicted, David can't reliably follow her.
If David can't be predicted, Christina can't reliably avoid him.
The question then becomes: what specific kind of randomization produces an equilibrium?
This is where the concept of mixed strategies becomes essential.

A mixed strategy is one in which a player doesn't always do the same thing, but instead assigns probabilities to their available actions and randomly selects among them according to those probabilities.
The question is what probabilities create a stable equilibrium — where neither player has any reason to change their randomization.
Think about whether it matters which probabilities they use, or whether there's a specific fifty-fifty split that is special.
Also think about question three from a symmetry standpoint: if the game is perfectly symmetric and both players randomize equally, what does that imply about the long-run payoffs?
And for question four, try to construct the mathematical argument showing that no change in strategy by either player can improve their expected value above what the equilibrium gives them.
These are challenging questions, and the next section will walk through all of them in full detail.
The tools you'll use are exactly the expected-value framework we just covered in the previous section.
